topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The postcard, glossy and smelling faintly of cheap ink, lay crumpled on my desk. Across the faded blue background, a picture of a sprawling, unfamiliar coastline dominated the frame. Liam. He was moving. Across the country. The silence in my apartment suddenly felt too loud, amplifying the frantic thumping in my chest. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over his contact, then letting it drop onto the worn wood. A strange tightness constricted my throat, making it hard to swallow. I started pacing the length of the living room, my footsteps echoing hollowly on the hardwood floor. Every corner of the room seemed to hold a memory of him—the way he’d lean against the doorframe, the sound of his specific laugh. Now, those anchors felt suddenly adrift. I kept staring at the framed photo of us from that disastrous camping trip last summer, the warmth of the memory curdling into something sharp and cold.

[story 2]

"So, you're actually doing it?" The words tasted like ash in my mouth. I kept replaying the conversation in my head, the casual way he’d mentioned the logistics, the easy smile that didn't quite reach his eyes. It was all so sudden, like a tectonic shift I hadn't braced for. I stood by the window, watching the familiar street below, the familiar rhythm of traffic suddenly feeling alien. My hands began to shake, a fine tremor that spread up my arms. I tried to rationalize it—a new job, a fresh start—but the logic felt flimsy, a thin veneer over a deep, churning anxiety. I walked into the kitchen, needing something solid to grip. I gripped the edge of the counter until my knuckles turned white, focusing intently on the chipped ceramic, trying to anchor myself against the dizzying sense of space opening up between us.

[story 3]

I reread the text message for the tenth time. It was just three sentences, yet they resonated with the weight of a thousand unspoken goodbyes. The sheer scale of the distance hit me like a physical blow, knocking the air out of my lungs. I sank onto the sofa, curling into a tight ball, pulling my knees to my chest. A dull ache started behind my eyes, a pressure building behind my temples. I stared blankly at the television, the colors on the screen blurring into meaningless streaks. Every sound—the distant siren, the hum of the refrigerator—was amplified, demanding attention in a way that felt exhausting. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the churning feeling down, but it only intensified, a heavy, suffocating blanket draped over my entire being.
